Scored per the criterion's own three-part definition; hospital-proximity/travel-time explicitly excluded here by the criterion's own definition — that's the separate distance-to-emergency-care fact-layer row. **(a) System capability: genuinely strong, one of this project's clearer positives.** Private hospitals (Hospital Alemán, Hospital Italiano, Hospital Británico, plus Sanatorio de los Arcos with its own trauma department and helipad) are described repeatedly as comparable to European standards with English-speaking staff — not a "good for the region" caveat, a real standalone strength. No specific medevac-line finding exists (what's genuinely not treatable in-country) — flagged as unresearched rather than assumed, in a major world city with this hospital density the practical medevac need is likely low, but that's an inference, not a sourced fact. **(b) Foreigner access route: real, multi-layered, better than it first looks.** Emergency care is free for literally everyone regardless of immigration status, no exception found (multiple 2026-07-11 sources). Scheduled/non-emergency public care is free for DNI-holding residents (temporary or permanent) on the same terms as citizens, but CABA specifically (plus four other jurisdictions) has billed non-DNI holders — tourists, transitorios — for scheduled public care since March 2025 (buenosairesherald.com/batimes.com.ar, corroborated by a baexpats.org community thread, checked 2026-07-11); temporary residents also lost *free* public-system access under Decree 366/2025 specifically, and must show private insurance to use it (reconfirmed against a second independent source this pass, not a reversal of what visa-legal.md already had). Private care has no residency-status gate at all — payable/insurable by anyone. **(c) Cost/insurability: cheap, and — the genuine surprise of this pass — explicitly NOT age-gated by law**, unlike the criterion definition's own general assumption (enrollment ceilings "commonly 65-75"). Ley 26.682 prohibits Argentine prepagas from rejecting any applicant for age or pre-existing conditions and mandates every prepaga offer its top age-bracket plan with no age ceiling, capping that bracket's premium at 3x the entry-bracket premium (argentina.gob.ar's own plain-language explainer, checked 2026-07-11) — a real, checked exception to the pattern the criterion was written expecting, not a silent match to it. Private prepaga insurance itself runs ~$50-150/month, genuinely cheap. **Real compound gates, named plainly, are why this isn't a 5:** Rentista (a likely-relevant pathway for a passive-income archetype, though this row is scored archetype/age-neutral) requires mandatory private health insurance with $20,000 minimum coverage as an approval condition (Decreto 366/2025, Confirmed-diverges, Medium confidence, already in visa-legal.md); separately, as of July 1, 2025, every foreign visitor of any nationality must show proof of travel/health insurance just to enter the country at all (enforcement inconsistent per multiple 2026 sources) — two distinct compound gates, one at the border, one at the visa-approval stage, stacked on top of an otherwise strong, affordable, legally age-protected system. Nets to 4: strong system capability, a genuinely strong and legally-protected insurability picture, real named access frictions for the specifically non-resident/newly-arrived window rather than a structural failure.

What genuinely is gated, on a real dated worsening clock: premium subsidies for lawfully-present immigrants under 100% of the federal poverty line ended 2026-01-01, and under H.R.1, Marketplace subsidy eligibility narrows to lawful permanent residents (plus a short named-exceptions list) starting 2027-01-01 — every one of visa-legal.md's real non-LPR routes loses subsidy eligibility on that date, converting the already-scored $687-750/month unsubsidized premium (Cost of living/Income viability rows above) from a worst-case number into the only realistic price point most Framing-B route-holders will ever. **(c) Cost/insurability:** the ACA Marketplace itself has no age-based enrollment ceiling (guaranteed issue regardless of age, unlike several other candidates' 65-75 private-market cutoffs) — a real structural strength, though largely moot for a working-age non-LPR holder, since Medicare itself stays closed to a non-citizen without LPR status regardless of age (no "aging into safety net" arc the way a citizen has). Per this project's climate-neutrality convention (the preference framework, 2026-07-07): no climate/biome preference is fixed, so this score reflects climate as fact, not a preference match. Cuenca's climate is genuinely mild and stable year-round (~20°C days, ~10°C nights, "eternal spring," climatestotravel.com/weatherspark.com, 2026) — a real, distinctive, high-altitude-driven profile, described factually rather than ranked.

Real and organized: 8,000-10,000 expats in a ~500-600k-person city, large enough to support informal networks for "virtually anything" (internationalliving.com, 2026). Held below 4 by two named, real frictions: a documented gringo-pricing pattern with a specific $292-vs-$50 pharmacy example and a sourced claim that locals are placing newspaper ads for $150/month rentals amid rising local rents (cuencahighlife.com, 2026), plus a named cultural-adjustment friction (Cuencan culture reads as close/family-oriented, a real difference some expats struggle with) and documented local resentment toward non-integrating expats (expatexchange.com, 2026).

Genuinely low across every category checked: $1,000-1,200/month single, $1,500-2,500/month couple (cost-of-living.md), a $2.50-3.50 full restaurant lunch, sub-$25/month internet. Held below 5 by one real, unresolved conflict: property purchase price-per-m² ranges from $500-650 (citywide) to ~$1,200 (Gringolandia-specific) depending on source, not yet reconciled (cost-of-living.md).

Added 2026-07-11 (13th criterion, research pass). **System capability:** a real, functioning public (IESS) + private tier, both physically present in Cuenca — a public university-affiliated hospital as the primary trauma/ER center, private hospitals running 24/7 ERs with faster triage (smilehealthecuador.com/red-flags.md, 2026), and Cuenca's own IESS hospitals specifically rated "generally better equipped" than IESS facilities in smaller Ecuadorian cities (expatecuador.com, 2026). **Reputation check, verified not transcribed:** Ecuador's WHO health-system ranking genuinely rose from 111th of 211 countries (2000) to 20th (2014), confirmed across academic sources (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Health_Organization_ranking_of_health_systems_in_2000, jabfm.org, ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/PMC, 2026) — a real, large improvement, but distinct from the vague "WHO ranked Ecuador 6th-most-improved" claim recirculating on expat-marketing sites (e.g. liveandinvestoverseas.com-style aggregators), which this pass could not substantiate and treats as an inflated/incorrect variant of the real underlying finding, not a fact in its own right. Cuenca's "retiree healthcare haven" reputation holds up directionally once separated from that one bad claim, not as a blanket confirmation. **Medevac line:** routine and moderately complex care handled in-city; complex/tertiary cases are stabilized locally then referred to Quito or Guayaquil, typically a short domestic flight via Cuenca's own Mariscal Lamar airport, not an international evacuation by default — full international medevac (US/UK/Canada) remains an option some expats/insurers plan for on the most severe/specialist cases specifically, at a cost of "tens of thousands of dollars" per flight (mti-247.com, 2026). **Foreigner access route — a genuine structural positive relative to private-only candidates:** legal residents (cédula-holders, meaning residency approval must already exist) can voluntarily affiliate with IESS, the public system, at $84.83/month minimum (17.6% of the $482 SBU base, 2026), rising toward roughly $264/month if IESS bases the contribution on a higher declared visa income (e.g. ~$1,500/month); full network coverage, no copays/deductibles, pre-existing conditions covered after a 3-month wait, dental and prescriptions included (cuencaexpat.com/myecuadorvisa.com, 2026). Tourist-visa holders cannot enroll — private insurance is the only route pre-residency. **Cost/insurability, including the compound age-gate the criterion's own definition asks for:** private insurers commonly set new-enrollment age ceilings — sources disagree on the exact number (60-65 per expatecuador.com vs. 65-70 per myecuadorvisa.com, both 2026, not reconciled this pass) — set against a 2017 law reportedly barring insurers from age-based rejection of *existing* clients; whether/how that protection extends to *new* enrollment above the common ceiling is unresolved this pass, not assumed either way. This creates a real, named compound gate: Ecuador's own income-threshold visa routes (Rentista, Pensionado, Digital Nomad) require proof of health insurance at the *application* stage, before a cédula — and therefore IESS eligibility — exists, so an older first-time applicant can hit a private-insurer age ceiling at exactly the moment coverage is needed to file, with no IESS bridge yet available (myecuadorvisa.com, 2026). Held at 4, not 5: the compound gate above is real and unresolved, complex care requires an in-country referral rather than being available in Cuenca directly, and none of this pass's healthcare claims were checked against a primary institutional source (IESS's own site, Ministerio de Salud Pública) — the same open primary-source gap named elsewhere in this file. Held at 4, not lower: the core system is genuinely functional, affordable, and offers residents a real public-system access route rather than private-insurance-or-nothing, which several other candidates in this project lack. Scored archetype- and age-neutrally per the criterion's own definition — the age-gate is named as a structural fact affecting a specific population, not used to lower or raise the score for an assumed reader.

A real, clean Digital Nomad visa route exists at $1,446/month for foreign-sourced remote work (visa-legal.md), and dollarization removes currency-conversion risk entirely for anyone earning/holding USD — a genuine structural advantage over every non-dollarized candidate in this project. Held at 3, not higher: **Ecuador's tax treatment of a resident's foreign-sourced income was not researched this pass** — unlike Paraguay's confirmed-permanent territorial exemption, this is a real, named gap, not a confirmed favorable answer. Scoring above 3 without checking this would be inventing a positive rather than finding one.

Median fixed-line download speed surpassed 93 Mbps as of mid-2024 per Ookla (expatecuador.com, 2026), with real fiber providers (Netlife, Xtrim) delivering low-latency service and plans up to 200+ Mbps. Held below 5: the underlying measurement is roughly 2 years old relative to this write-up, not a fresh pull, and Cuenca-specific coworking-space depth wasn't independently checked this pass.

Full fee-simple foreign ownership under Constitutional Article 321, with Cuenca sitting well outside both real restricted zones (the 50km border band, the ~8km coastal band) — a clean legal mechanism (visa-legal.md). Held below 5: the same property-price conflict noted under Cost of living, plus **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — no first-hand account of a completed Cuenca purchase, clean or contested, was found this pass.

Not lake/ocean-adjacent — a genuinely different shape from several other candidates. A genuine positive, but urban-river-plus-nearby-páramo is a different (and this pass judges, slightly less immersive for daily life) profile than direct lake/ocean access — held at 3 rather than higher pending a first-hand account of how residents actually use this access day-to-day.

Dollarization is a genuine, distinctive structural positive — no currency to devalue, 1.5% 2026 inflation forecast credited directly to it (allianz-trade.com, 2026). Held at 3, not higher, by two real, current, named findings: Noboa's four-question constitutional referendum was rejected on all four counts in November 2025 (his first major electoral defeat, government described as "weakened" afterward, latinoamerica21.com/constitutionnet.org, 2025), and sustained executive-Constitutional Court tension plus states-of-exception use tied to the security crisis (latinoamerica21.com, 2026) — a real rule-of-law caveat distinct from the crime rate itself, which is scored separately under Safety.

No compound or co-living precedent exists in Cuenca itself — Ecuador's two named intentional communities (Chambalabama near Vilcabamba, Dos Tortugas near Jama) both sit well outside Cuenca (permies.com/ic.org, 2026), and one source explicitly notes "very few facilities in Cuenca and Ecuador, and fewer still meet the standards expats might hope for" (internationalliving.com, 2026). The large, organized expat community is a real informal-support positive but doesn't itself substitute for a documented small-group/compound precedent — scored the weakest criterion this pass, honestly, not padded by the community strength above.

The national picture is genuinely severe — Ecuador has been in a declared state of internal armed conflict since January 2024, ~51/100,000 national homicide rate, among the highest in Latin America (gordoninstitute.fiu.edu, 2025). Cuenca itself reads at a starkly different **1.4/100,000** with no local state of emergency (cuencaexpathub.com, 2026) — a real, large, directionally-credible gap. Held at 3, not higher, because that 1.4 figure rests on a single expat-guide site citing unspecified "government data," not independently cross-checked against Ecuador's own police/INEC statistics this pass — a real number, not yet a fully verified one.

Held at 3, not higher: a real, named high-turnover pattern cuts both ways (easy to join, but a shallower long-term social fabric), no group/compound precedent was found, and no first-hand multi-year account exists.

Genuinely the cheapest location in this entire project's research to date — a single-person total cost estimated around $463-538/month, meaningfully below Belize's Corozal (this project's prior budget benchmark) and dramatically below Cape Town (~$2,092/month) (cost-of-living.md). Not raised to 5: a real, named affordability-erosion trend (rising rents, steep supermarket prices relative to local wages) and the EGP's own severe historical volatility (down 70%+ since 2022, though currently stabilizing) both temper the headline cheap-price claim — this is cheap today, with a real, live currency-risk asterisk attached.

scored 2026-07-11 (13th-criterion research pass package). A genuine three-tier system, not a single national fact: Dahab's own facility (Dahab Medical Care Hospital, private) covers routine care, diving injuries, and severe infections, but not complex surgery/cardiology/oncology; a real credentialed, JCI-accredited general-hospital tier exists in Sharm El-Sheikh; genuine tertiary/complex specialist capability (cardiac, cancer, transplant, all JCI-accredited) exists in Cairo (cost-of-living.md#healthcare-costs-system-capability-and-access-route). Distance/travel-time between these tiers is deliberately excluded here per this criterion's own definition — it's fact-layer in red-flags.md's emergency-care section, not scored. Foreigner access route: private is the real, practical door — Egypt's public Universal Health Insurance System is optional for foreigners and gated on reciprocal agreements or formal local payroll employment, neither of which fits a typical non-employed foreign resident's shape, and public-sector quality is independently poor even for citizens (cost-of-living.md). Cost/insurability: local/regional expat plans run ~$500-2,000/year; no Egypt-specific enrollment-age ceiling was confirmed this pass, but the general international-insurer pattern these same providers (AXA, Allianz, Cigna, Bupa) operate under elsewhere lands at 65-75 for new enrollees — an unconfirmed-for-Egypt but realistic operating assumption. A real compound gate found this pass, not previously surfaced: the real-estate-investment residency route (already uncertain for Dahab given the unresolved leasehold-eligibility question) itself requires proof of medical insurance plus a medical exam/health certificate as part of the application — a double gate for an older applicant if Egypt's own insurers do carry the general age-ceiling pattern. Nets to 2, not lower: genuine tertiary capability is reachable within-country (unlike a true medical desert), and the access-route/cost picture reads as typical-for-this-project rather than uniquely punitive — but local capability is genuinely thin and the foreigner-access default is private-only, the same shape driving several of this file's other below-middle scores. Archetype- and age-neutral: this note names the general age-gating pattern as a fact for any older reader, not a claim about any one reader's own age (no citation exists in this project's own preference or financial-fit assessments for that).

A real, structural weak point: measured internet speeds (4G 10-25 Mbps, café WiFi 5-15 Mbps with peak-hour drops) are the weakest found across this entire pass, well below Cape Town's measured 48 Mbps citywide figure, and Starlink is categorically unavailable/illegal to operate (red-flags.md) — a genuine remote-work-viability caution, not a minor inconvenience. No income-tax-residency research was done this pass either, a real gap. Not scored lower: the very low cost of living (~$463-538/month single-person estimate) meaningfully reduces the income bar needed to sustain a routine here, a real, partial offset to the connectivity weakness.

The weakest score in this file, and the sharpest, most specifically-sourced finding of this pass: measured 4G speeds of 10-25 Mbps and café WiFi of 5-15 Mbps with real peak-hour drops (red-flags.md) — genuinely the lowest measured connectivity figures found across this entire project's candidates to date. Starlink is not legally available and importing hardware is restricted, a more categorically closed position than Belize's contested-but-live regulatory fight over the same technology. Emergency care requires a real ~1-hour-plus ambulance transfer to Sharm El-Sheikh for anything beyond routine/dive-specific care, with Cairo medevac for the most severe cases — a real structural gap, though not the worst found in this project (Belize's cross-border dependency is comparable in shape).

The sharpest structural finding of this entire pass: **foreigners cannot hold freehold title to any property in Dahab at all** — Law 14/2012 and Presidential Decree 128/2022 specifically bar foreign ownership across Sinai, including Sharm El-Sheikh, Dahab, and the Gulf of Aqaba (cost-of-living.md, visa-legal.md). The only available instrument is a long-term (up to 99-year) lease — real and "widely used," but a fundamentally different, weaker legal position than every other location in this project, including Mexico's/Guatemala's restricted-zone trust structures, which at least grant a real ownership-equivalent vehicle. This is a direct, structural conflict with a cash-purchase plan specifically. Scored at the project's lowest Land/property figure found to date, below even USA's Framing-B visa row, because the mechanism itself — not just the income/threshold fit — is closed to foreigners here. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — whether the 99-year leasehold functions as securely as first-hand accounts casually describe, or carries its own inheritance/renewal-dispute risk, was not verified.

Genuine, specific, real strength: direct shore access to Red Sea coral reef, one of the world's best-regarded freediving/shore-diving locations (Lighthouse/El Fanar reef specifically), plus a distinct kite/windsurfing draw at the Laguna (overview.md). Not raised to 5: no water-quality or reef-health data was independently sourced this pass, and the desert-coast setting means no green/inland-nature dimension to complement the marine strength the way Cape Town's mountain-and-coast combination offers.

A real, ongoing currency and fiscal fragility: the EGP has lost 70%+ of its value since 2022, external debt service due in 2026 alone is $27 billion against a $15 billion projected current-account deficit, with interest payments exceeding half of total government expenditure (red-flags.md, cost-of-living.md) — a genuinely more fiscally stressed picture than any other candidate in this pass, though improving under IMF oversight and Gulf-state support (inflation down from ~24% to ~12-13%). The Sinai-specific foreign-ownership ban (2022) is itself a real, recent, tightening-not-loosening rules-of-the-game fact, distinct from the safety question. No 2026 political-transition event was found, and Egypt's political system reads as stable-but-authoritarian rather than actively destabilizing — this score reflects the currency/fiscal fragility and the property-rights tightening, not a claim of regime instability.

No specific precedent for small-group/compound-style settlement (per this project's group-viability framing) was found or searched for this pass (community-network.md), and the same Land/property restriction above would apply to any group scenario as much as an individual one — a real, structural constraint on this criterion specifically, not just an unresearched gap. Scored at 2, matching Cape Town's same-pass score for the same "genuinely unresearched, not confirmed poor" reason, with the added structural property-access ceiling named explicitly.

A genuinely laid-back, dive-centered daily rhythm is real and consistently described across first-hand-adjacent nomad-guide sources (overview.md, community-network.md) — "easy to forget you're in Egypt," per one account. Held at 3, not higher: the same high-turnover pattern flagged under Community above raises a real question about whether the town supports a genuine 3-year-plus routine or mainly a shorter nomad-season stay, and a real, named "decline in affordability" trend (rising rents, steep supermarket prices) is a live headwind against the town's own cheap reputation.

A genuinely precise, dual-sided finding, not a blanket read: Dahab itself suffered a real, serious mass-casualty terrorism incident in 2006 (24 dead), and sits on the same peninsula as an ongoing, separate North Sinai insurgency — but current UK FCDO/multi-government travel advisories explicitly exclude Dahab's own coastal zone from the restricted-travel areas, and no dated incident inside Dahab has been found since 2006 (red-flags.md, checked directly against primary government travel-advisory sources, not aggregator reputation alone). Real, current, lower-severity risk (petty theft, Bedouin-security tension) is also named. Nets to 3, not lower: this project's own charter is to name risk plainly without letting a historical or regional reputation overstate a currently-bounded, dated risk — the precise, sourced picture here reads as genuinely more moderate than "worst regional profile of the ten" would suggest on its own, while still real and worth full weight.

No live digital-nomad or passive-income-based long-term visa exists (visa-legal.md) — a genuinely thinner visa landscape than most of this project's other candidates. The one real long-term mechanism, real-estate-investment residency ($50k-$200k tiered), runs into Dahab's own Sinai-specific foreign-ownership ban: whether a 99-year leasehold (the only property instrument foreigners can hold here) qualifies for this residency program was **not confirmed this pass** — the single highest-priority open question in this file. The 30-day tourist visa (extendable, with a real 14-day overstay grace period) is a genuinely workable short-stay bridge but doesn't convert to anything longer-term on current evidence. Nets to 2: a real, if narrow and unconfirmed, long-term mechanism exists, distinct from a confirmed structural dead end, but meaningfully weaker than South Africa's comparable row this same pass. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked.**
Reported factually: warm, mild Mediterranean climate, broadly similar to Valencia's. Held below 5 for the same reason as Valencia — not independently checked against a primary meteorological source.

Real, dense, mature infrastructure exists (a weekly nomad meetup running continuously since 2020, Poblenou's tech/coworking-dense 22@ district) — but this criterion has to weigh the organized, physically confrontational, escalating anti-tourism backlash squarely: two consecutive years (2024, 2025) of coordinated water-pistol protests against tourists, the second explicitly cross-city-coordinated, against a backdrop of ~70% rent growth and ~40% home-price growth over the past decade (`community-network.md`, `red-flags.md`). Scored per this criterion's continuum (system capability, foreigner access route, cost/insurability including age-gated private insurance — explicitly excluding hospital-proximity/travel-time, which lives in the distance-to-emergency-care fact row in the underlying research instead). **System capability**: Spain's SNS ranks among the world's best per a WHO assessment (the underlying research, aggregator-sourced, not independently traced to WHO's own primary ranking this pass); Valencia's own facilities are real and named — Hospital Universitario y Politécnico La Fe (public), Quirónsalud, 9 de Octubre, and Vithas (private, the latter running a dedicated International Patient Care service). **Foreigner access route**: real but genuinely gated, not free-and-immediate — the NLV (the passive-income route) requires private health insurance covering 100% of costs with zero copayments as a hard visa-issuance condition (the underlying research, primary-institutional, exteriores.gob.es); public-system access only opens after 12 months' legal residency via the voluntary pay-in Convenio Especial (€60/month under 65, €157/month 65+, cross-corroborated), which itself excludes subsidized prescription rates — not free entitlement even after years of residence. **Cost/insurability**: a real, age-gated compound gate — private insurance runs roughly €50-200/month generally but rises steeply with age (~€250-400/month around 70, €350-600+ beyond), and most mainstream insurers cap new enrollment between 65 and 75 (Sanitas 75, Adeslas 70, Asisa 69), though a minority extend further (ASSSA to 79, Cigna no upper limit) — genuinely workable at any age with deliberate insurer-shopping, not a hard wall, but a real compound cost since private cover isn't optional for an NLV applicant. Held at 4, not 5: strong system capability and a genuinely workable access route, offset by the real age-gated insurance compound and the buy-in-only (not free) shape of public access under the NLV specifically. Archetype- and age-neutral: described for any reader's age/income shape, not scored favorably or unfavorably by assumption.

Scored archetype-neutrally per this project's standard (the income archetype is still an open decision-framework question) — not against the Non-Lucrative Visa's threshold specifically, which is the Visa & legal pathway ease row's job, not this one. The clearest gap, weighted honestly rather than papered over: no research exists on Valencia's local job or small-business market specifically — if the eventual archetype turns out to be on-location (a café, in-person tutoring), this file currently has nothing to say about it. Not evaluated this pass — a dense urban Mediterranean city is likely a structurally different shape than the standalone-house/compound model in this project's group-viability framing, but this wasn't independently checked for Valencia. Honest gap, not a confirmed negative.

Valencia reads as the calmer of Spain's two locations (`overview.md`), but the 2024 DANA catastrophic flood event — while concentrated in satellite towns 8+ km from the city center, not the center itself — is a real, serious, regional weather hazard this project should weigh with the same seriousness given to Guatemala's volcanic risk or Lisbon's earthquake exposure (`red-flags.md`). The deepest, most mature digital-nomad/expat community infrastructure found in any candidate this project has researched — 20+ coworking spaces in Canggu alone, established multi-year meetup and online-group culture, genuinely differentiated sub-communities (Canggu's remote-work crowd, Seminyak's resort crowd, Ubud's wellness crowd, Uluwatu's surf crowd) rather than one blended identity (`community-network.md`).
