scored 2026-07-11 per the 13th criterion's adoption; scored per its own three-part definition — proximity/travel-time excluded by the criterion's own design, stays under Safety. **System capability:** real for routine/intermediate care — BIMC/Siloam/Kasih Ibu handle minor surgery, fractures, dengue monitoring, maternity, pediatrics, and general specialist consultation, with BIMC Kuta running 24hr A&E and a 3-bed ICU — but the medevac line is clearly and consistently drawn: major cardiac surgery, severe trauma, complex neurological procedures, and specialized cancer treatment route to Singapore, and Bali has only 1-2 JCI-accredited hospitals versus Bangkok's 60+, a real quantified capability gap relative to the region's medical-tourism hub. **Foreigner access route:** the load-bearing finding — BPJS (the public system) is confirmed unavailable to Retirement KITAS holders and to Second Home Visa holders (both non-working/non-employer-sponsored routes), the two routes this project's own Visa & legal pathway ease row already treats as Bali's realistic fits; clear BPJS eligibility exists only for an Indonesian-employer-sponsored working KITAS, a route already found structurally unavailable to this project's passive/remote income archetypes. Private/international insurance reads as the real door for this profile, not a preference — a genuine access constraint, not just a cost one. One source conflict flagged, not resolved. **Cost/insurability:** age-gated on both insurance tiers, and the gating compounds with Bali's own visa shape — local Indonesian private insurers commonly stop new enrollment at ages 55-65 (harsher than the 65-75 range typical elsewhere in this project), while Retirement KITAS itself carries a 55+/60+ age floor, meaning someone first qualifying for the visa is already at or past the local insurer's own cutoff, pushed toward the pricier international tier (~€2,000/yr at 60, enrollment to ~75-80) from day one rather than as a later-life transition. Both the Second Home Visa and E33G list valid health insurance as a named application requirement, not an optional add-on (E33G specifically requires international coverage, travel insurance excluded). **Net:** real, competent private-sector infrastructure and a short, known evacuation path (2-2.5hrs to Singapore) keep this off the bottom of the scale, but the structural BPJS exclusion for every realistic route plus the earlier-than-typical local-insurer age ceiling are genuine, current frictions, not hypothetical ones — landing at the middle rather than above it. Archetype- and age-neutral: scored for any reader's visa/insurance shape, not assuming a retiree. **Paper-vs-practice divergence flag: Not yet checked** — no first-hand account found of a Second-Home-Visa or Retirement-KITAS holder's actual insurance/access experience.

Scored archetype-neutrally per this project's standard (the income archetype is still an open decision-framework question) — not against the Second Home Visa's lump-sum passive-wealth route specifically, which is the Visa & legal pathway ease row's job, not this one. Worth stating plainly and honestly the way Thailand's Chiang Mai row does for the DTV, and it cuts the opposite direction here: Indonesia's Second Home Visa is unusually generous across archetypes for a single route — explicitly a non-working visa that nonetheless permits remote work for foreign companies (visa-legal.md) — so it doesn't force a choice between Passive and Remote the way most single-purpose passive-income visas elsewhere in this project do. But every route reviewed (Second Home, E33G, Retirement KITAS) structurally excludes local Indonesian-client income or local employment — an On-location archetype (a local business, in-person tutoring for local clients) reads structurally unsupported by any legal route found this pass, a genuine cross-archetype gap worth naming here as an income-viability fact, not filed away as a visa-mechanics footnote. On the ground: Bali carries the deepest, most mature remote-work infrastructure of any candidate in this project (20+ coworking spaces in Canggu alone, fiber 50-300 Mbps — though backup generators built into coworking spaces imply an imperfect underlying power-reliability baseline, `overview.md`), and cost of living is genuinely affordable ($1,800-2,500/month comfortable in Canggu/Seminyak, meaningfully cheaper in Ubud), extending runway for any income shape. Two real, current frictions temper this rather than a clean high score: the 10-25% annual rent-growth trend in Canggu/Uluwatu/Seminyak specifically is worsening, not stable (`cost-of-living.md`); and the 2026 enforcement crackdown's widened "unauthorized work" definition is producing a documented chilling effect on exactly the kind of online self-presentation (content creation, brand partnerships) that a Remote-archetype income is often actually sustained through day to day (`community-network.md`) — a real, current risk to the income question itself, distinct from the separate Routine-sustainability and Visa-row concerns it also touches. Neither Remote nor Passive gets a fully clean, risk-free path here, and On-location gets none at all — the honest reason this doesn't score higher despite real infrastructure and cost strengths.

Canggu specifically has real, sourced, strong fiber infrastructure (50-300 Mbps depending on tier) and a dense coworking network with backup-generator redundancy — the generator detail itself implying an imperfect power-reliability baseline worth naming, a different infrastructure floor than this project's EU candidates (`overview.md`). Held at 3, not higher, for that reason and because Ubud/Uluwatu/Sanur weren't independently speed-tested this pass.

The single lowest-scoring row in this file, and deliberately so: foreigners cannot hold freehold title to Bali property at all — a fundamentally different, harder starting position than every other candidate in this project, all of which permit some form of direct foreign freehold ownership. Layered on top: an estimated 10,500 Bali properties currently sit in illegal, court-voided nominee arrangements, and a new 2026 regional regulation (Perda 4/2026) just added criminal exposure on top of the existing civil unenforceability (visa-legal.md, property.md). The three legal routes (leasehold, Hak Pakai, PT PMA) are real and workable, but this is a genuinely harder, more structurally constrained picture than any other candidate's Land/property row in this project — scored to reflect that honestly, not softened for Bali's popularity.

A genuine, undiluted strength: tropical beaches, surf culture (Uluwatu), rice-terrace/jungle immersion (Ubud) — no countervailing water-quality or access issue found this pass. The clean top score this project reserves for an unambiguous case.

A genuinely mixed national picture: President Prabowo has positioned Indonesia as a stable, pro-investment "calm bet," and general conditions show political stability with no massive human-rights-violation pattern — but 2026 has also seen the rupiah become Asia's worst-performing currency, the Jakarta Composite Index fall to its lowest level since May 2021, and an increasing military/police footprint in senior political positions (red-flags.md). No currency-crisis-level event on the scale of Argentina's peso history, but a real, live, currently-unresolved tension between stated posture and concrete economic-confidence signals — scored at the middle to reflect genuine unresolved direction, not a settled read either way.

Not evaluated this pass — and worth naming that this question likely needs its own framework rather than a direct port of this project's freehold-based group-viability reasoning, given Bali's non-freehold property structure (`community-network.md`). Honest gap, not a confirmed negative or positive.

The 2026 Dharma Dewata Patrol enforcement operation — a 100-person task force actively patrolling Canggu and Seminyak specifically, with a deliberately widened "unauthorized work" definition reaching even unpaid promotional content — is a real, current, escalating source of day-to-day uncertainty for exactly the population and neighborhoods a foreign remote worker would occupy (red-flags.md, visa-legal.md). Scored low deliberately, the same discipline this project applies to Barcelona's active backlash and Lisbon's own tension finding, though the underlying mechanism here (targeted immigration enforcement, not social backlash) is a genuinely different shape.

Violent crime against foreigners is genuinely low, multiple sources describing it as lower than many major Western cities (`red-flags.md`). Held below 5 because Numbeo's own composite safety index sits around 50 (driven by phone-snatching, ATM skimming, and — named as a distinct, real hazard — traffic accidents), and because a genuine, serious risk (methanol poisoning from unregulated local spirits) sits adjacent to this criterion even though it's homed under Alcohol culture rather than Safety directly.

The Second Home Visa's 3-year PR conversion is a genuine structural strength, but this criterion has to weigh the concrete, currently-escalating enforcement environment squarely: a 100-person task force, 62 detentions in three weeks, and a widened work-activity definition create real, live compliance risk specifically for anyone whose status isn't airtight — a materially different and more acute risk than any EU candidate's visa-mechanics friction in this project. **Paper-vs-practice divergence flag: Not yet checked** for the legal routes themselves, though the enforcement-volume finding itself is well-documented, not speculative.

A genuine three-season profile (hot pre-monsoon, heavy monsoon with real infrastructure closures, a mild dry season) rather than a flat "always warm" read (`overview.md`) — reported factually per the project's climate-neutrality convention, no claim made about fit to any fixed preference, since that convention doesn't scope this to Goa specifically. Scored at the methodology's neutral midpoint pending an explicit preference signal, not as a judgment on the climate itself.

A real but genuinely thin remote-work-specific community relative to this project's other Southeast Asia/Latin America candidates: the dominant social texture is party/backpacker culture, not a nomad-professional fabric, and a "strong professional or nomad community is lacking" per the one aggregator source that named this directly (bucketlistbri.com, 2026). The Anjuna-Assagao corridor does have 3-4 real coworking spaces and a genuine cross-timezone remote-work crowd (`community-network.md`), which keeps this off a 1, but no first-hand multi-year account was found to confirm depth or integration ease — a real, named gap.

Genuinely cheap by this project's standards: $600-1,000/month comfortable-lifestyle range, Anjuna-Assagao 1BHK rent ₹12,000-25,000/month (~$145-300) (`cost-of-living.md`). Held below 5 because Numbeo's own sample is thin (15 contributors) and no second independent aggregator cross-check was completed this pass — a real, named confidence gap, not a documented cost problem.

scored 2026-07-11 per the 13th-criterion adoption. **System capability, checked at Goa's own level rather than assumed from India's national medical-tourism reputation:** real specialist depth exists in-state — Manipal Hospitals Goa (robotic surgery, named cardiology/neuro/ortho/GI/oncology programs) and Victor Hospital, Margao (South Goa's only cardiac cathlab, current-generation neuro/spine surgical tech) — genuinely more than "a hospital exists," but the state's own Goa Mediclaim Scheme recognizes Mumbai referral hospitals (Tata Memorial for cancer, P.D. Hinduja) alongside in-state facilities, a real government-acknowledged ceiling on Goa's own tertiary depth for the most complex cases — a short (~1hr flight) medevac line, not a severe one, by this project's standards (overview.md). **Foreigner access route — this is where the visa finding bites directly:** private-pay treatment is the real, functional door (no residency/citizenship gate, foreign-patient services genuinely exist), but this project's own already-confirmed finding that India has no long-stay visa route at all for this profile means whoever is actually using that access is realistically on a capped, non-continuous tourist e-visa (30-day non-extendable, or up to 180 days/year on multi-year e-visas) — never a settled resident, and never eligible for any public/subsidized access route, because none exists to attach to a residency status that doesn't exist here. Access stays private-pay and structurally interrupted for as long as this profile holds, not a gap that closes with time the way it might on an actual residency track. **Cost/insurability:** genuinely cheap private care, and a real outlier finding relative to this criterion's own 65-75-ceiling framing — India's domestic private insurance market had its maximum entry age removed entirely by IRDAI directive effective April 1, 2024 — but this doesn't clearly resolve for this project's actual reader, since no source confirms a non-resident foreign national can even buy that domestic product; the realistic route (renewable short-term travel/medical insurance) was checked instead and holds up, including for older travelers. **Net score:** a genuine mid-tier 3, not a floor score — private healthcare in Goa is real, decent, and affordable, distinct from the property/visa criteria's hard walls, but access is never continuous or public-fallback-backed given the no-long-stay-visa finding, which is the dominant reason this doesn't score higher. Does not fold in hospital-proximity/travel-time (already scored under Safety's distance-to-emergency-care fact, per this criterion's own exclusion).

No passive-income or digital-nomad visa route exists in Indian law at all (`visa-legal.md`) — the sharpest income-viability constraint in this file. Internet reliability is a named, real problem even in coworking spaces (`overview.md`), a genuine practical drag on remote income delivery distinct from the visa question. Not a 1 only because the Anjuna-Assagao corridor's real coworking infrastructure and cross-timezone remote-work community show it's *possible* in practice for people already doing it — but the legal foundation for staying long enough to make that sustainable is absent.

Real, named unreliability, not just a slower-than-ideal speed: internet reliability is described as a major issue even inside coworking spaces (`overview.md`) — a more serious, functionally-limiting finding than a low Mbps number alone would be, since it affects consistency of remote-income delivery specifically. No direct measured speed-test data was found this pass to quantify the gap further.

The sharpest, most severe finding of this entire pass: a foreign national of non-Indian origin, resident outside India, **cannot purchase any immovable property in India at all**, under any circumstances, short of inheritance from an India-resident decedent (`visa-legal.md`). This is categorically stricter than every other candidate in this project's roster, including Vietnam's leasehold-with-quota model — India permits essentially no purchase path at all for a reader with no OCI/NRI status. A `HardConstraintRule`-shaped fact, scored at the floor deliberately, not a soft continuum. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked.**

Genuine beach/coastal access is the core of Goa's identity — this wasn't independently re-verified with dedicated sourcing this pass (a real gap, distinct from Belize's or Thailand's dedicated water-adjacency writeups), but nothing found this pass contradicts Goa's well-established beach-destination character, and no source suggested a Corozal-style "not actually a beach town" surprise. Held below 5 for the sourcing gap, not for a documented downside.

A genuinely mixed, not one-directional, picture: the Modi government sits at its strongest political position since 2019 (21 states ruled directly or in coalition, ~75% of the population) — real concentration and continuity at the top. But a separately-sourced, current economic-fragility narrative runs alongside it: only $3B in net FDI in the first nine months of FY2025-26, described as an active "investment strike," and a flat-to-declining national investment rate since 2014 (`red-flags.md`). The rupee is actively, successfully managed (RBI reserves over $700B) rather than under acute pressure. Nets to a genuine middle score — real political durability, real and current foreign-investment hesitancy, neither dominant enough to move it off-center.

Directly downstream of the Land/property access finding above: with no legal purchase path available, any group settlement model would have to run entirely on long-term rental, a structurally more precarious foundation for a multi-year group settlement than any freehold-purchase-based candidate in this project offers (`community-network.md`). Not independently tested against a real rental-only group scenario this pass, but the structural constraint alone justifies a floor score consistent with the Land/property finding it flows from.

The tourist-assault trend (`red-flags.md`: 45 assault cases since January 2026, up 30% from 2024, 60% targeting women) and the tourist-visa cap structure (180 days/year even on a 5-year e-visa) both work against "a life, not a waypoint." The Anjuna-Assagao corridor's real coworking/wellness infrastructure (yoga, cafes, community) is a genuine positive counterweight, keeping this off a 1, but the underlying legal/safety instability for a long, settled routine is real and current, not speculative.

A real, current, quantified negative trend, not just petty-crime baseline risk: Goa Police's own 2026 data shows a 30% year-over-year rise in tourist assaults, 60% targeting women, alongside a documented police-responsiveness gap on violence against women (`red-flags.md`). At least one source frames Goa as having become a genuine "crime capital" within India — a harsher characterization than the tourist-assault statistics alone, included rather than smoothed over. Petty theft is the baseline risk on top of this. No Numbeo index was pulled this pass to cross-check quantitatively — a real gap that could move this score in either direction on a follow-up pass.

The lowest score this file can honestly give, matching USA's project-wide floor for the same reason: no researched route fits a foreign national with no Indian-origin ancestry, no Indian spouse, and no employer/large-capital investment. The 30-day e-visa is single-entry/non-extendable; the 1-year/5-year e-visas cap any single stay at 180 days/year; Employment/Business visas require an employer or short-term-only activity; OCI (the one route offering something durable) is legally barred to anyone without Indian-origin ancestry or an Indian spouse. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — no lived account found either confirming or diverging from the legal picture.

Not independently researched in depth this pass — a real, named gap (`overview.md`), the same honest gap as Da Nang's climate row. The general hot, humid, equatorial-proximity profile is widely known but wasn't verified with dated temperature/rainfall figures. Scored at the methodology's neutral midpoint pending real research.

A genuinely English-speaking, welcoming, long-established expat/retiree community (`community-network.md`) — a real structural advantage over Goa's and Da Nang's non-English-first environments. Held below 4 because the community found this pass skews retiree-heavy rather than nomad/remote-worker-specific — no coworking-centered nomad district comparable to Goa's Anjuna-Assagao or Da Nang's An Thuong was confirmed to exist, a real, named open question rather than a documented absence.

The least expensive of this session's three candidates on the specific rent metrics cross-checked (`cost-of-living.md`): Numbeo 1BR city-centre RM 1,647.50 (~$350-390) and outside-centre RM 938.75 (~$200-220), both below Da Nang's and Phuket's own Numbeo figures. Held below 5 because Numbeo's own sample (23 contributors) is middling, no second independent aggregator was fetched this pass, and alcohol specifically is a genuinely expensive line item (`red-flags.md`) that a blended cost figure could understate for anyone who drinks regularly.

scored 2026-07-11 — the 13th criterion, per the criterion's own definition: (a) system capability, (b) foreigner access route, (c) cost/insurability; hospital-proximity/travel-time explicitly excluded (fact-layer only, see distance-to-emergency-care). **(a) System capability — re-verified, not restated, and extended to the public system for the first time:** the private/medical-tourism layer holds up fully on re-check (Gleneagles Penang 380-bed JCI+MSQH, Island Hospital, Penang Adventist; Penang MHTC-designated, 2026 = Malaysia's "Year of Medical Tourism") — genuinely strong. Newly confirmed this pass: Penang General Hospital is the **public tertiary referral hospital for northern Malaysia**, not a minor clinic, with an active KKM-backed expansion underway (overview.md). No Penang-specific medevac line (what exactly isn't treatable locally) was confirmed — only the generic ASEAN regional pattern (complex neurosurgery/major trauma/advanced oncology sometimes routed to Singapore), a real, named gap. **(b) Foreigner access route — genuinely dual, not private-only:** foreigners are not barred from the public system (unlike some candidates in this project) but pay the full unsubsidized non-citizen rate — roughly RM40 vs. RM1 (citizen) for outpatient, RM100-120 vs. RM3 for specialist consultation, raised again in 2026 — still cheap in absolute USD terms (~$9-27) but a real, worsening-trajectory multiplier; private care carries its own new 6% non-citizen SST as of 2026 (government/university hospitals stay SST-exempt for everyone) (overview.md). In practice, most non-Malaysians default to private care given public wait times — the private/medical-tourism layer is the realistic primary door, with public as a genuine but second-choice backstop. **(c) Cost/insurability — the age-gated compound gate this pass specifically asked to check, found and named precisely:** MM2H requires Malaysia-approved medical insurance for applicants under 60 before visa endorsement; 60+ gets a case-by-case assessment with a real Immigration Department exemption path if coverage genuinely can't be secured — but Malaysian local private insurers commonly stop taking new enrollees around age 60-70 (some cap at 70-75), pushing older applicants toward international insurance (commonly extends to ~75) (visa-legal.md). This is a real third gate on top of MM2H's already-documented deposit-plus-income-plus-property structure, not the same gate restated. **Archetype/age-neutral:** stated as the route's own structure, for any reader, not scored "for a retiree." Held to 4, not 5, for two concrete gaps: no confirmed Penang-specific medevac line, and no lived account of the 60+ insurance-exemption process in practice. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** (insurance-exemption process specifically).

The DE Rantau Nomad Pass is a genuine, real remote-work visa route — the only one of this session's three candidates with a purpose-built digital-nomad visa at all (`visa-legal.md`) — but it requires $24,000/year in *active* overseas employment/freelance income, unconfirmed whether passive income qualifies, and no coworking/nomad-district infrastructure was confirmed to exist in Penang the way it was in Goa and Da Nang. No internet-speed data was gathered this pass either — a real, compounding gap. Nets to a real but genuinely uncertain middle-low score, not a confirmed strength.

The weakest-researched infrastructure picture of this session's three candidates: no internet-speed data (advertised or measured) was gathered this pass at all (`overview.md`), a real, named gap distinct from Goa's and Da Nang's files, both of which at least surfaced advertised-plan figures. Scored low specifically for the research gap, not because any negative finding was made — a genuine "not yet checked" rather than a documented weakness, but scored conservatively per this project's standing convention of not rounding an unconfirmed gap upward.

A genuinely workable, if state-gated, purchase path: foreigners can buy both freehold and leasehold property, subject to state-set minimum price thresholds (`visa-legal.md`) — a real, more permissive structure than India's near-total bar, though gated by a real, sharp within-Penang divergence (RM 1M/RM 3M on the island vs. RM 500k/RM 1M on the mainland) that a single "Penang" figure would obscure. State-government approval is required on top of the price threshold, a real added friction. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — no lived account of the approval process or threshold enforcement was found this pass.

Real beachfront living exists (Tanjung Bungah, Batu Ferringhi) alongside George Town's heritage-urban character (`overview.md`) — genuine variety across the island, but not independently researched in depth this pass (reef/water quality, swimmability) the way this project's dedicated water-adjacency writeups elsewhere are. Scored as a real but under-researched positive.

A genuinely stable political picture overall — Malaysia's Global Peace Index placement and general "little ongoing upheaval" characterization are real and well-corroborated (`red-flags.md`) — but a real, named counter-current exists: at least one dedicated political-analysis source frames PM Anwar Ibrahim's unity-government coalition as genuinely "unstable," a tension not resolved by this pass either way. Nets to a genuine middle score: durable in practice so far, with a real, sourced fragility concern that keeps this from a higher score.

Penang's price-threshold-gated but otherwise conventional freehold/leasehold purchase mechanism could in principle support an adjacent-parcels or same-building group-settlement model more readily than Vietnam's 30%-quota system or India's outright bar (`community-network.md`) — not independently tested against a real group scenario this pass, a real, named gap. Scored as a plausible-but-unconfirmed middle position, consistent with Vietnam's own scoring logic on this same criterion.

Malaysia's firm "no extensions, no tolerated border-run culture" tourist-visa posture (`visa-legal.md`) is a real structural drag relative to Vietnam's currently-tolerant border-run environment — but MM2H (once qualified for) offers a genuinely more durable, multi-tier long-stay structure than anything found for India or Vietnam, and Penang's retiree-oriented community suggests real precedent for people actually settling long-term here. Nets to a real middle score reflecting both the entry-gate friction and the durable-once-in structure.

A genuinely strong, well-corroborated finding: Malaysia ranks 13th most peaceful country globally and 2nd in Southeast Asia on the Global Peace Index, with Penang specifically not carrying a reputation for serious attacks on tourists/residents (`red-flags.md`) — the strongest safety reputation of this session's three candidates on the qualitative evidence gathered. Held below 5 because no Numbeo or equivalent quantified index specific to Penang was pulled this pass to back the strong qualitative read with a number.

The most structurally *interesting* of this session's three visa pictures, not the worst: MM2H is a real, working long-stay program (unlike India's or Vietnam's complete absence of a passive-income route), but "deposit-gated" undersells its real 2026 shape — a dual gate of tiered fixed-deposit-plus-income thresholds (RM 150,000/RM 5,000-per-month for Silver; RM 500,000/RM 10,000-per-month for Gold) *plus* a newly-compulsory qualifying property purchase (`visa-legal.md`). This is a real, workable route for someone who clears the Silver tier's numbers, but the compulsory-property-purchase addition is a genuinely more demanding 2026 structure than the label implies, and whether any MM2H tier converts to permanent residency was not confirmed this pass. Scored above India's and Vietnam's 1s because a real, if demanding, route exists at all — not scored higher because the actual numbers and PR-conversion question remain partly unresolved. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked.**

Per this project's climate-neutrality convention (the preference framework, 2026-07-07): no climate/biome preference is fixed, so this score reflects climate as fact. Asunción's climate is humid subtropical with real seasonal extremes — mild winters (18.7°C average) versus hot, muggy summers (28.8°C average, daytime highs commonly 38°C, heat waves to 40°C) and no true dry season (climatestotravel.com/weather-and-climate.com, 2026) — a genuinely different, more extreme profile than Cuenca's stable mild year-round climate, described factually rather than ranked against it.

The honest, named finding: the expat/digital-nomad scene here is real but genuinely small and "still developing" — "no massive co-living spaces, limited expat events, and few 'nomad services'" (expatsettle.com, 2026), a direct, named contrast with Cuenca's larger, more established scene. One source frames it more optimistically ("a large and growing community with frequent get-togethers," nomads.com, 2026) — a real, unresolved tension named rather than resolved in either direction, but the weight of evidence points to genuinely thinner social infrastructure than most of this project's other candidates.

Genuinely low, at or below Cuenca's figures: $1,000-1,500/month single, comparable family-of-four costs undercutting both Montevideo and (on some lines) Buenos Aires (benoitproperties.com, 2026). Held below 5: property price-per-m² data rests entirely on real-estate marketing/aggregator sources, not a government transaction registry, and no independent Numbeo/Expatistan-vs-government cross-check has been run (cost-of-living.md).

Added 2026-07-11 (this research pass, 13th criterion, weight Medium — definition in this project's own scoring methodology). A genuinely mixed picture across all three of the criterion's components. **(a) System capability:** a real three-tier structure — free MSPBS public hospitals that read genuinely thin ("emergency care facilities in local government hospitals are not reliable," overcrowded, outdated equipment, movetoparaguay.com/planbexpat.com, 2026), a better-resourced IPS semi-public tier, and a private tier that handles real complex care locally, not just routine visits (cardiac surgery $8,000-25,000 vs. $80,000-200,000 US, described as approximating regional US hospital standards — goldenharbors.com, 2026). For genuinely severe conditions, "many expats simply fly to Brazil or Argentina for treatment" (planbexpat.com, 2026) — a real, active air-ambulance industry serving Asunción (Silvio Pettirossi Int'l Airport) confirms this medevac line is genuinely serviced, not a hypothetical gap (airmedical.com, 2026). **(b) Foreigner access route:** a genuine, unusually generous positive — MSPBS public healthcare is free to any foreigner with valid ID regardless of residency status — but the better IPS tier sits on an employment-contribution gate that structurally excludes Paraguay's own target passive-income visa holders (Rentista, Pensioner). **(c) Cost/insurability:** genuinely cheap by USD standards (GP $25-50, specialist $30-80, hospital day rate $80-200) and local private insurance is cheap too ($30-100/month, or $1,500-3,500/year for a 60-70 couple at a higher tier) — but the compound age-gate the criterion is explicitly built to catch is real and confirmed: local insurers commonly cap new enrollment at 65-70, pushing anyone older to international insurers (Cigna/Allianz/IMG) at $4,000-8,000/year for a couple, roughly double-to-triple the local rate; existing enrollees keep coverage past the cutoff, so the gate bites only at entry, not on renewal. Whether the Rentista/Pensioner visa itself requires proof of insurance is genuinely conflicting across sources, unresolved. Net: a cheap, unusually foreigner-open-at-the-free-tier system with real, named limits at the complex-care and insurance-entry-age edges — not "particularly bad" by this criterion's own bottom-of-scale test (nothing here resembles "must evacuate for anything beyond routine care" or "no functioning system"), but genuinely short of a clean strength. Excludes hospital-proximity/travel-time by definition — see the distance-to-emergency-care fact row in the fact-layer instead.

The standout finding of this whole pass: Paraguay's territorial tax system exempts foreign-sourced income (remote work, dividends, rental income, capital gains, pensions) from Paraguayan taxation **permanently, with no time limit** — a genuine, confirmed structural advantage, stronger than Uruguay's time-limited equivalent (overview.md/visa-legal.md). Held below 5: the Rentista/Pensioner visa's ~$1,500/month figure is a practitioner benchmark, not a confirmed fixed statutory threshold the way Ecuador's SBU-indexed number is — a real, named sourcing gap.

A real, freshly-measured strength: Claro delivered an average measured 174.2 Mbps download / 135.2 Mbps upload over the July 2025-June 2026 period (speedgeo.net, 2026) — notably fresher and higher than Cuenca's mid-2024 Ookla figure. Held below 5: no Asunción-specific coworking-space depth was independently checked this pass.

The cleanest foreign-ownership regime found anywhere in this project so far: Law 117/91 grants foreigners the same property rights as citizens — no residency requirement, no trust/nominee structure, no restricted-zone exception touching Asunción at all (the one real restriction, Law 2532/2005's border-zone rule, applies only to bordering-country nationals' rural land purchases, not Asunción, and not most passport profiles) (visa-legal.md). Held below 5, not because of the legal mechanism itself, but because Paraguay's Corruption Perceptions Index score (24/100, 150th of 182 countries, Transparency International 2025) is a real, quantified institutional-quality caveat worth weighing before assuming a clean statute always plays out cleanly in practice — **paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked**, no first-hand purchase account found either direction.

Real, developed, but urban-park in character, not nature-immersion: the Costanera de Asunción, a 15km riverside promenade on the Paraguay River with green space, bike lanes, and daily local use (evendo.com/aroundus.com, 2026). No swimming, reef, or hiking-access profile was found this pass — a genuinely thinner nature offering than Cuenca's páramo/lake access or a coastal candidate's beach access, named as a real gap rather than assumed absent.

The weakest-scored criterion this pass, and deliberately not softened: Paraguay's Corruption Perceptions Index score is 24/100 (150th of 182 countries, Transparency International 2025), and the BTI's own 2026 assessment states plainly that "the rule of law remains weak and there has been little progress in fighting corruption" despite real democratic stabilization since 1989 (bti-project.org, 2026). Former president Cartes' continued informal political influence — despite (and even after the October 2025 lifting of) US sanctions, with one source describing him as still effectively "the real president... holding court around the grill" — is a real, live, named institutional-power question, not settled history, and is credited with deepening intra-party divisions and legislative gridlock (americasquarterly.org, 2026). A real, recent reform (Law 7389/24, a national anti-corruption integrity framework) is genuine but too new to have a track record. No currency/reserve-ratio data was pulled for the floating Guaraní this pass — a real gap relative to Ecuador's dollarization finding.

A genuinely mixed, honestly-scored finding. The structural mechanism is about as favorable as this project has found: raw Chaco-region land runs $50-100/hectare (liveandinvestoverseas.com, 2026), and Law 117/91 removes any legal foreign-ownership obstacle. But the one documented attempt at exactly this project's small-group model — a permaculture-village project seeking like-minded co-settlers — was **cancelled outright from insufficient interest**, down to just two committed people (permies.com, 2026) — a real, honest null result on lived precedent, not a null search. Nets to a genuine opportunity with no track record behind it, scored in the middle rather than either direction.

Not dedicatedly researched this pass beyond climate and safety texture — a real gap, not a confident score. The climate itself (see Climate row) includes genuine seasonal extremes (summer heat waves to 40°C) that plausibly affect daily routine and pace differently than Cuenca's stable year-round mildness, but this wasn't independently tested against lived-account evidence this session.

A genuinely strong national picture: 6.2/100,000 homicide rate (lower than Uruguay, Brazil, Colombia), a US State Department Level 1 advisory (the least severe tier), 4th-most-peaceful country in South America per the 2025 Global Peace Index (workwealthandtravel.com/globalwealthprotection.com, 2026) — the strongest hard safety number found in either of this pass's two candidates. Held below 5 by real, named, non-trivial concerns: motochorro phone-snatching, police corruption at traffic stops (a real institutional-quality issue, distinct from street crime itself), a genuinely elevated road-death rate (22.6/100,000, 78% motorcycle-involved), and year-round endemic dengue.

A genuinely complete route set: Rentista, Pensioner, a fast SUACE investor route (permanent residency directly, no temporary phase, 1-6 months for a ~$70,000 in-business investment — the fastest route to permanent status found anywhere in this project so far), employer-sponsored work permits, and family/student routes (visa-legal.md). The overstay penalty is also strikingly light (~$36, 48 hours to leave) relative to Ecuador's ~$788/2-year-ban. Held below 5: no primary institutional source (Dirección General de Migraciones, SUACE itself) was directly checked this pass, and the core income thresholds are practitioner benchmarks, not confirmed fixed statutory figures.

Not independently researched in depth this pass — a real, named gap (`overview.md`). The general tropical-monsoon pattern with a typhoon-exposed wet season (roughly September-December) is widely known but wasn't verified with dated figures. Scored at the methodology's neutral midpoint pending real research, not as a judgment on the climate itself — this is the most honestly under-researched climate score of this session's three candidates, flagged plainly rather than estimated.

An Thuong reads as a genuinely concentrated, purpose-built nomad district (multiple coworking spaces, work-friendly cafes, wellness infrastructure, all within a few walkable blocks) — more compact than Goa's multi-village Anjuna-Assagao spread or Phuket's family-enclave-driven neighborhood split (`community-network.md`). Held below 4 because no first-hand, multi-year account was found to confirm whether this reads as an easy-to-integrate-into community or a closer-knit, harder-to-break-into scene — a real, named gap.

A solid, well-sourced (68-contributor Numbeo sample, the strongest of this session's three candidates) cross-check landing at $700-1,300/month all-in comfortable range, with An Thuong-specific rent ($250-500/month) sitting at or below the citywide average (`cost-of-living.md`) — genuinely moderate, not cheap the way Goa is, and Numbeo's own page flags possible staleness relative to current market conditions, a real caveat worth naming.

scored 2026-07-11 (healthcare-criterion package) — scored per the criterion's own three-part continuum definition, archetype- and age-neutral, hospital-proximity excluded by definition (that's the separate distance-to-emergency-care fact row, already present in the fact-layer). **System capability:** genuinely good for routine/standard care — Vinmec Da Nang (JCI Gold Seal-accredited) and Hoan My both deliver Western-standard care across most specialties plus a real 24-hour ER, but Da Nang lacks the sub-specialist depth of Hanoi/HCMC; complex oncology, rare neurological disorders, and complex surgery generally require medevac to Singapore, Bangkok, or home country ($15,000-50,000+ USD). **Foreigner access route:** private-pay is the real door — expats are ineligible for subsidized public insurance and can only use public facilities at full non-subsidized cash rates; work-permit holders' compulsory social insurance (VSS) is public-hospital-only with low reimbursement, "essentially irrelevant" for expats using private care in practice. No visa/TRC route found this pass makes insurance a condition of the visa itself (unlike Thailand's O-A mandate) — moot for a passive-income reader anyway since no such route exists here at all (visa-legal.md). **Cost/insurability:** private out-of-pocket care is genuinely affordable by this project's standards (a specialist consultation runs ~€54-126, a CT/MRI ~€98-325), and private/international insurance is available across a wide price band (~$200-4,547+/year depending on scope) — but carries a real, region-typical age-gated new-enrollment ceiling (most insurers cap between 60-70, with some extending to 75-76; coverage renews indefinitely once enrolled, the ceiling only gates starting it). Nets to a genuine middle score: a real, JCI-accredited private system handles routine and standard-emergency care well and affordably, but structurally has no public-system safety net for foreigners and a hard ceiling on treating complex/specialist conditions locally — neither a standout strength nor a named floor.

No passive-income or retirement visa route exists (`visa-legal.md`), the same structural gap as India and most of Southeast Asia in this project — but Vietnam's infrastructure genuinely supports remote income better than Goa's: residential fiber at 280-310 Mbps for ~$7/month, and 50-100 Mbps standard in coworking spaces/cafes (`overview.md`), a real, positive contrast to Goa's named reliability problems. Nets to a real middle score: strong delivery infrastructure, but no legal route that actually fits a purely passive-income profile long-term.

A genuine, confirmed strength: 280-310 Mbps residential fiber for ~$7/month is among the strongest advertised-value broadband figures found anywhere in this project's roster (`overview.md`), and coworking/cafe speeds (50-100 Mbps standard) are solid and consistent, unlike Goa's named reliability problem. Held below 5 because no direct measured (as opposed to advertised-plan) speed-test data was gathered this pass.

A genuinely distinctive middle position, neither India's near-total bar nor a clean freehold system: foreigners can hold a 50-year renewable leasehold (up to 100 years total) on an approved building unit, capped at 30% foreign ownership per building (`visa-legal.md`) — a real, working purchase path, but structurally different from and more limited than Guatemala's or Belize's freehold model. Da Nang specifically is named as one of Vietnam's most open cities for foreign buyers. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — no lived account of the quota system or resale-lease-inheritance mechanic was found this pass, the same open question already flagged for Thailand's comparable condo-quota system before a lived account surfaced there.

Real, genuine beach access (My Khe) directly adjacent to the An Thuong nomad district — a real positive, but not independently researched to the same depth (reef quality, water clarity, seasonal swimmability) as this project's dedicated water-adjacency writeups elsewhere. Scored as a real but under-researched positive, not a confirmed standout.

A genuinely durable one-party state (Communist Party of Vietnam) with real, structural political stability — but that stability comes from suppressed dissent and centralized control, not competitive consensus (`red-flags.md`). A real, current 2026 leadership consolidation (Tô Lâm holding both General Secretary and President simultaneously, an unprecedented combination) reinforces predictability at the top. Foreign investment is a genuine, current draw (rising China-plus-one dynamics, $4.4B US FDI stock), offset by real, named frictions (tariffs, weak IP protection, regulatory opacity). Nets to a genuine middle score: durable and predictable at the top, with real governance-model and named-friction caveats that keep this from a higher score.

The leasehold-quota system shapes but doesn't foreclose a group model the way India's outright bar does: a group of friends could plausibly buy multiple units in the same building (subject to the 30% cap not already being filled), a genuinely different "settling together" shape than adjacent-land-parcel models elsewhere in this project (`community-network.md`). Not independently tested against a real group scenario this pass — a real, named gap, scored as a plausible-but-unconfirmed middle position rather than assumed to work smoothly.

Vietnam's currently-tolerated, un-crackdowned border-run culture (`visa-legal.md`) is a real structural advantage for sustaining an actual routine relative to Thailand's 2025-2026 tightening — but the complete absence of any long-term legal status built for a passive-income foreign national (no TRC route fits) caps how sustainable a multi-year routine can genuinely be without either an employer, an investment, or a Vietnamese spouse.

A genuinely strong, consistently-corroborated finding across multiple independent sources: Da Nang is widely regarded as the safest, most livable city in Vietnam, with violent crime described as extremely rare (`red-flags.md`). The real, named risk is traffic/scooter-accident-related, not crime — a materially different (and arguably more addressable, via choosing not to self-drive) risk profile than most of this project's Latin American or even other Southeast Asian candidates. Held below 5 because no Numbeo or equivalent quantified index was pulled this pass to back the strong qualitative consensus with a number.

Confirmed directly this pass: Vietnam has no retirement visa, no passive-income route, and no digital-nomad visa (`visa-legal.md`). Every real TRC route requires employment, registered investment, or a Vietnamese spouse — none of which describes a reader with no Vietnamese employer/spouse and a purely passive income. Whether any TRC route even converts to Permanent Residence remains a genuinely open, unconfirmed question, an additional structural uncertainty on top of the entry-gate problem itself. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** on the property-leasehold mechanism specifically.

A genuine Mediterranean climate — warm dry summers (~26-27°C), cool wet winters (~18°C) — a real, distinct profile from every other active candidate in this project, none of which run a true winter-rainfall pattern (overview.md). Per this project's climate-neutrality convention (the preference framework, 2026-07-07), scored as a fact of real seasonal variety and moderate year-round temperatures, not assumed to match or clash with any stated preference. Not raised to 5 given the real, named Cape Doctor wind pattern as a genuine outdoor-living/small-craft factor, and no first-hand comfort account exists.

A real, organized digital-nomad/remote-work community exists (Nomad Week Cape Town, January 25-31 2026; 100+ coworking spaces; coworkingsafari.com's "one of the strongest digital nomad bases in Africa" framing) plus a much longer-established general expat population — genuinely more infrastructure than a cold-start candidate. Held below 4: no specific expat-population size/retention data was found, and no first-hand multi-year account exists to confirm the community holds up past the nomad-scene layer (community-network.md).

Genuinely mid-tier, not budget-tier: Observatory/Woodstock rent ($350-550/month for a 1BR) is real and meaningfully cheaper than Sea Point ($500-2,400/month), and the city overall reads roughly 30% below major US cities per one aggregator (cost-of-living.md). Held at 3, not higher: no verified line-item monthly budget exists, the single total-cost figure ($2,092/month) is single-sourced and its methodology unconfirmed, and the floating, historically volatile Rand carries a real, untracked exchange-rate risk this pass didn't quantify.

Scored per the 13th criterion's own definition — (a) system capability, (b) foreigner access route, (c) cost/insurability — explicitly excluding hospital-proximity/travel-time, which lives in red-flags.md's distance-to-emergency-care fact instead. **(a) System capability, quantified rather than restated as a vague positive** (re-derived from source, not taken from this file's own prior "strong private emergency healthcare" phrasing): South Africa's private sector is Africa's strongest — Numbeo Health Care Index 64, highest of any African country (statista.com, 2026) — and functions as a genuine *regional* medical hub: patients are more often evacuated **into** South Africa from elsewhere on the continent than out of it (medical-air-service.com, gmsrs.com, both 2026). Three groups (Netcare — 57 hospitals; Mediclinic Southern Africa — 50 hospitals + 15 day clinics + 6 mental-health facilities; Life Healthcare) cover ~80% of the private market, several JCI/COHSASA-accredited, with confirmed local capability for complex procedures — cardiac surgery (Cape Town's own Groote Schuur performed the world's first heart transplant, 1967) and joint replacement, at costs well below US-equivalent (heart bypass ~$50,000 in SA vs. $144,000 US; hip replacement $12,000-15,000 vs. $30,000-40,000 US — medigence.com, vaidam.com, both 2026, evidence of real capability, not just claimed prestige). Set against a real, structural public/private gap, not a minor caveat: public hospitals (used by roughly 80-84% of the population, sources vary 16-20% on the private-use share) are described as overcrowded, understaffed, and under-resourced, quality ranging "acceptable to very low" (wisemove.co.za, expatica.com, both 2026) — South Africa still ranks only 49th of 94 countries on one separate global composite healthcare index despite the private sector's strength (clarity.africa, 2026 — a different product from Numbeo's index, not cross-checked against it this pass). **(b) Foreigner access route, named precisely:** legally more open than most candidates researched in this project — a foreign national legally resident on a valid visa/permit is constitutionally entitled to use public facilities on the same income-sliding-scale subsidy as citizens, and anyone regardless of status can receive emergency hospital care (sadag.org, sihma.org.za, both 2026) — but an income-qualifying visa holder (Retired Person's or Remote Work Visa tier) sits well above the subsidized bracket and would pay close to full price even inside the public system, so **private is the real functional door on cost/quality grounds, though not a legal bar** the way some candidates' public systems close outright to foreigners. Tourists specifically are not eligible for free public care (out-of-pocket or travel insurance only). **(c) Cost/insurability — genuinely more favorable than this criterion's own commonly-seen pattern, worth naming as a real positive divergence:** South Africa's Medical Schemes Act (1998) legally bars medical schemes from age-discriminating in acceptance — **no confirmed maximum enrollment age** was found for a domestic medical aid scheme (medicalaid.com, keyhealthmedical.co.za, both 2026), unlike the 65-75 enrollment-ceiling pattern this project has found elsewhere. A late-joiner premium surcharge applies to anyone first joining after age 35 (permanent, not a barrier), and premiums do rise informally with age. Comprehensive international-style expat health insurance runs roughly $100-500/month, commonly cited near $200/month (expatica.com, william-russell.com, both 2026) — moderate, not prohibitive. **A real compounding gate, though not age-based:** the Retired Person's Visa — visa-legal.md's most-likely-fit route for a passive-income archetype — requires comprehensive private health insurance valid in South Africa for the visa's full duration as a hard condition (travel insurance explicitly not accepted; citizenremote.com, ibn.co.za, both 2026); no source found this pass confirms whether that required policy itself carries any age ceiling. Nets to 4, not 5: quantified private-sector strength plus an unusually open insurability picture (no confirmed age ceiling, a genuine positive relative to this project's usual pattern) outweigh the real gaps — the functional door for an income-qualifying foreign resident is private-only in practice even though not legally barred, no primary StatsSA/Department of Health source was checked, no first-hand expat account of actually using the system was found, and exactly "where the medevac line sits" (what specifically isn't treatable locally) wasn't pinned down beyond South Africa's general regional-hub reputation. Archetype- and age-neutral: scored as facts true for any reader, not adjusted for a retiree persona.

GMT+2 time-zone alignment with European business hours is a real, structural remote-work advantage (overview.md, community-network.md), and the Remote Work Visa's legal existence is itself a real positive relative to candidates with no nomad-visa mechanism at all (Egypt, this same pass). Held at 3, not higher: the visa's own income floor (~$39,500/year) is a genuinely high bar for a passive-income archetype, and South Africa runs residence-based worldwide-income taxation once a stay crosses 183 days — a real, structural tax exposure most of this project's territorial-tax candidates (Belize, Guatemala) don't carry.

A real, dated recovery on both historic crisis fronts: 365 consecutive days without load-shedding as of 2026-05-16 (Eskom, gov.za) and dam levels at 77.5% (2026-07-07), comfortably above the 2018 Day Zero threshold (red-flags.md, overview.md) — genuinely stronger infrastructure trajectory than this file expected walking in, given the city's global reputation for both crises. Measured citywide internet (48.04 Mbps via 400+ free Wi-Fi zones) is real but modest; individual coworking spaces advertise materially faster (100-200 Mbps fiber). Held at 3, not higher: both recoveries are recent and explicitly caveated by their own sources as not-yet-guaranteed-permanent (the City's own "one storm does not make a wet year" messaging), and the water-reduction program still covers over half a million households nationally, not fully resolved everywhere.

A genuinely open regime on paper: no general foreign-ownership ban, no city-level foreigner quota, no foreigner-only property tax (visa-legal.md, cost-of-living.md) — closer to Belize's full-freehold model than to Mexico's/Guatemala's restricted-zone trust structures, and real foreign-buyer activity is already happening (R1 billion+ in five months of 2025). Held at 3, not higher: the 2024 Expropriation Act's without-compensation provisions are a real, live, judicially untested legal question with no source confirming whether foreign-owned property is treated any differently — a genuine, unresolved uncertainty, not a confirmed problem, but also not a clean 4-5 the way an unambiguously open regime would earn. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — no first-hand account of a foreign buyer's actual purchase-and-ownership experience was found.

Genuinely strong and varied: Atlantic coastline, Table Mountain and the Southern Suburbs' green belt, the nearby Winelands — a real, structurally different nature profile from every tropical/Caribbean candidate in this project. Not raised to 5: no dedicated first-hand or hard-data nature/water-quality check was run this pass, the same gap most first passes in this project carry before a dedicated rescore.

A genuinely more actively contested environment than several of this project's other candidates: the 2024 election ended 30 years of ANC single-party rule, producing a real, still-settling 10-party Government of National Unity with named friction over tax/land/education policy between the ANC and DA (red-flags.md, Wilson Center/Bloomberg 2026). The Expropriation Act is a real, live rules-of-the-game question specifically relevant to property rights, not yet resolved either reassuringly or alarmingly (a real court test case is scheduled February 2026). No confirmed currency crisis, capital-control event, or foreign-ownership rollback was found this pass, and the Rand's recent appreciation is named as a real, if not precisely quantified, investor-confidence signal. Nets to 3: real friction and one genuine unresolved legal question, but not a confirmed instability event.

No specific precedent for small-group/compound-style settlement (per this project's group-viability framing) was found or searched for this pass (community-network.md) — a real, named gap, not a confirmed poor fit. The Southern Suburbs' likely greater house-not-apartment inventory is a plausible lead, not yet confirmed with property-type data. Scored conservatively at 2 given the genuine absence of evidence either way, the same treatment this project gives an unresearched-rather-than-confirmed-poor criterion — worth a dedicated look before trusting this number, per this project's mandatory positive-lever-hunt convention on a criterion this thin.

A genuine Mediterranean-climate, four-neighborhood-texture city (overview.md) offers real variety for a sustainable daily routine — café/coworking culture in Observatory, promenade life in Sea Point, quiet suburban life in the Southern Suburbs. Held at 3: the crime-concentration nuance (Safety row, red-flags.md) means routine planning has to account for real, if avoidable, risk zones (the airport-road carjacking pattern specifically), and no first-hand lived account confirms day-to-day sustainability the way a multi-year resident's own words would.

A genuinely sharp, real split: Cape Town ranks 16th globally on Numbeo's perception-based Crime Index (a real caveat on that index's own methodology), but official SAPS precinct data shows Constantia (Southern Suburbs) at 100/100 vs. Camps Bay at 48/100 — and the large majority of gang-driven violent crime concentrates in the Cape Flats townships, areas no expat-relevant neighborhood in this file's scope overlaps with (red-flags.md). The one named, location-specific risk for tourists/expats directly is airport-road carjacking — real, but avoidable with informed transfer choices. Nets to 3: genuinely elevated at the national/city level, genuinely more reassuring once read at the neighborhood level actually in play, with no area-specific homicide rate found to fully quantify either read.

A genuine two-tier picture, scored on the harder number: the Remote Work Visa requires ~$39,500/year, a genuinely high bar for a passive-income-only archetype (visa-legal.md) — not a fit. The Retired Person's Visa's ZAR 37,000/month (~$2,000 USD) threshold is meaningfully more realistic, but whether ordinary passive/investment income (versus a named pension/annuity instrument) qualifies was **not resolved this pass** — the same recurring open question this project has hit in Belize's, Guatemala's, and Colombia's equivalent research. A real "slow yes" to Permanent Residency exists under Section 27(e) if the R37,000/month income is provably lifelong — a genuine structural positive, but conditional on an income-type question still unresolved. Nets to 2, not lower, because a real, plausible-fit route exists pending one confirmable question — not a confirmed structural dead end the way USA's Framing-B research found. **Paper-vs-practice divergence: Not yet checked** — no first-hand account of a foreign buyer's or visa-holder's actual experience was found this pass.
